# Changelog

## [Unreleased]

### Fixed

- **`finalize()` in `createRequestLogger` now wraps all logging work in a try/catch** (`src/request-middleware.ts`): A throwing `enrich` callback, `messageBuilder`, function-form `level`, or `logger.log` call previously propagated out of the `res` `finish`/`close` event listener, causing an `uncaughtException` and a process crash — violating the package's "logging is never the cause of a request failure" invariant. The `res.removeListener(...)` calls run unconditionally before the try block so the double-fire guard always cleans up; on catch, a single bare `console.error` records the failure (mirroring `attachTransportErrorHandler` in `src/logger.ts`) without going through the logger itself (to avoid recursion). Non-Error thrown values are stringified via `String(err)`. Four new tests in `tests/request-middleware.spec.ts` assert that each throw site (`enrich`, `messageBuilder`, `logger.log`, function-form `level`) produces no propagated exception and exactly one `console.error` call.
- **Function-form `level` return value is now validated at request time** (`src/request-middleware.ts`): Previously, a `level: (statusCode) => ...` callback returning an invalid string or `undefined` was passed directly to Winston, producing a `[winston] Unknown logger level` warning and silently dropping the log line — violating the package's "logging is never the cause of a request failure" invariant. The `validateRequestLevelOption` JSDoc incorrectly claimed the return was "checked at request time" when no such check existed. Now, after the callback fires inside `finalize()`, the return value is validated with `isValidLogLevel`; an invalid or `undefined` return falls back to `determineLevel(statusCode)` so the log line is always emitted at a well-formed level. The JSDoc on `validateRequestLevelOption` has been corrected to accurately describe this runtime fallback. Three new tests in `tests/request-middleware.spec.ts` assert that invalid and `undefined` returns produce a logged entry at the correct status-derived level (2xx → `"info"`, 4xx → `"warn"`, 5xx → `"error"`) and that the log entry is never dropped.
- **`maxBodyLength` is now validated up front** (`src/errors.ts`, `src/request-middleware.ts`): Previously, passing `NaN` as `maxBodyLength` silently disabled the body-size guard (huge payloads were logged in full without warning), and `0` or a negative value produced an empty-preview envelope on every request — both silent misconfigurations invisible to the caller. The new `validateMaxBodyLength` helper (added to `__requestInternals`) runs at `createRequestLogger()` call time, BEFORE the `loggingEnabled`/`loggingMode` short-circuit, and throws `RequestLoggerOptionError({ code: "INVALID_BODY_LIMIT" })` for any value that is not `undefined`, a positive finite number, or `Infinity`. `Infinity` is accepted as "no limit" (body is always logged in full). The new `"INVALID_BODY_LIMIT"` code has been added to the `RequestLoggerOptionErrorCode` union in `src/errors.ts`. Six new tests in `tests/request-middleware.spec.ts` assert that `NaN`, `0`, `-5`, and `"100"` (wrong type) each throw with `code === "INVALID_BODY_LIMIT"`, that a valid number passes, and that `Infinity` passes and correctly skips truncation for a 4 000-character body.

## [0.21.9] - 2026-06-30

### Security / Dependencies (2026-06-30)

- **Closed all three open Dependabot alerts** by pinning the affected transitive **devDependencies** to their patched versions via a new top-level `overrides` block in `package.json`. All three are build/test-time-only transitive dependencies — none is a runtime dependency and none ships in the published tarball (the `files` allowlist is `dist`, `README.md`, `LICENSE`), so consumers of `@hiprax/logger` were never exposed; the alerts only ever concerned the maintainer's CI and local dev toolchain. `npm audit` now reports **0 vulnerabilities at every audit level** (was 1 moderate + 2 low). Full pre-completion checklist passes (build, 253/253 tests + 100% coverage on every metric, lint, type-check, format:check).
  - **`js-yaml` → `^3.15.0` (override `"js-yaml@3"`)** — closes **GHSA-h67p-54hq-rp68 / CVE-2026-53550** (moderate; quadratic-complexity DoS in YAML merge-key handling via repeated aliases). Reached only through `ts-jest > @jest/transform > babel-plugin-istanbul > @istanbuljs/load-nyc-config`, which parses the project's own trusted nyc/istanbul config during the coverage step of `npm test`; the package never parses untrusted YAML. The `@3` selector key scopes the override to the js-yaml **3.x line only** so a future 4.x consumer is never forced down to 3.x (verified against npm 11's version-range override semantics).
  - **`@babel/core` → `^7.29.7`** — closes **GHSA-4x5r-pxfx-6jf8 / CVE-2026-49356** (low; arbitrary file read via crafted `//# sourceMappingURL=` comment, CWE-22). Pulled in only through jest / ts-jest's transform plumbing, where Babel only ever processes the project's own first-party source; the advisory itself notes that "users that only compile trusted code are not impacted."
  - **`esbuild` → `^0.28.1`** — closes **GHSA-g7r4-m6w7-qqqr** (low; Windows-only arbitrary file read in esbuild's HTTP development server, CWE-22). Reached only through `tsup` for one-shot bundling; esbuild's dev server (`serve` / `--servedir`) is never started by this package, so there is no request surface to traverse. `tsup@8.5.1` (the latest release) still declares `esbuild ^0.27.0`, so an `overrides` pin is the only way to reach the fix while keeping `tsup` on its latest version; the build and full test suite confirm `tsup@8.5.1` is fully compatible with `esbuild@0.28.1` (dual ESM/CJS + declaration output unchanged). `package-lock.json` updated to the patched resolutions (`js-yaml@3.15.0`, `@babel/core@7.29.7`, `esbuild@0.28.1` plus its platform-specific `@esbuild/*` packages); `npm ci` confirmed in sync.

### Packaging (2026-06-30)

- **Stopped publishing the raw `src/*.ts` sources** (`package.json` `files`, `.npmignore`). Removed `src` from the `files` allowlist, trimming the published tarball from **15 files to 9** (`LICENSE`, `README.md`, `package.json`, and the six `dist/` artifacts) and the unpacked size from ~558 kB to ~430 kB. The raw sources were redundant for debugging: the `dist/*.map` sourcemaps already inline the complete original TypeScript via `sourcesContent` (verified), so debuggers resolve original source from the maps alone, and no declaration maps (`.d.ts.map`) are emitted — meaning a shipped `src/` would not have powered "go to definition" from the type declarations either. The package's only public entry is the `.` export (there is no `./src` subpath in the `exports` map), so omitting the sources breaks **no** consumer import path; this is a packaging-only change with no API or behavioral impact. The `.npmignore` header and a new defensive `src/` entry were updated to document the new published set and keep the sources out even if the allowlist is ever broadened.

## [0.21.8] - 2026-06-30

### Packaging (2026-06-30)

- **Added `.npmignore`** (`.npmignore`): a defensive second layer over the existing `package.json` `files` allowlist (`dist`, `src`, `README.md`, `LICENSE`), which remains the primary control over what gets published. The published tarball is unchanged at 15 files (`LICENSE`, `README.md`, `package.json`, the six `dist/` artifacts including sourcemaps, and the six `src/*.ts` sources); `npm pack --dry-run` confirms no test, config, script, CI, or local tooling file is included. Because the `files` allowlist force-includes the `dist` and `src` directories, the ignore file does not change current contents; it documents what must never ship and keeps stray cruft (test files, coverage, caches, OS / editor files, env files, logs, local `*.tmp` backups, packed `*.tgz`, and the local orchestrator tooling) out of the tarball even if the allowlist is ever broadened or removed. The `.ts` sources and `.map` sourcemaps are intentionally retained so consumers can resolve original source while debugging.
- **`.gitignore`** (`.gitignore`): added a dedicated "Orchestrator" section so local orchestrator tooling never enters version control (`.orchestrator/`, `orchestrator/`, `.orchestrator.ps1`, `.orchestrator.env`, `orchestrator.ps1`, `orchestrator.env`, `phases.json`, `PLAN.md`, `plan.md`).

## [0.21.7] - 2026-05-12

### Documentation (2026-05-12)

- **`README.md` badge row trimmed.** Removed the `Dependencies` badge (Libraries.io release status for `@hiprax/logger`) from the header row. The remaining badges retain their existing order: npm version → npm provenance → License → CI → CodeQL → codecov → Node.js.

### Security / Code Scanning (2026-05-12)

- **Closed all three open CodeQL `security-and-quality` alerts on the repo.** The CodeQL scan that runs on push to `main`, on PRs targeting `main`, and on a weekly schedule was reporting three open findings in the Security tab. None had a known runtime impact (each was either statically mitigated by surrounding code or operated on a narrowly trusted input), but all three were silenced at the source so the Security tab is now clean. Full pre-completion checklist passes (build, 253/253 tests + 100% coverage on every metric, lint, type-check, format:check). `npm audit` continues to report 0 vulnerabilities at every audit level.
  - **`js/polynomial-redos` — HIGH severity — `src/logger.ts:324` (alert #3).** The trailing-hyphen anchor `[-]+$` inside `sanitizeSegment` matched CodeQL's canonical polynomial-regex shape (a quantified, anchored repetition that backtracks across every candidate start position when the string does NOT end with the quantified character). The runtime exposure was theoretical — the preceding `.replace(/-+/g, "-")` already collapses every run of `-` to a single character, so the anchored regex only ever sees a string with no adjacent hyphens and cannot exhibit the polynomial behavior — but CodeQL's local taint flow does not see that contextual mitigation. Replaced `^[-]+|[-]+$` with `^-|-$` (no `+` quantifier), which is provably linear on any input and semantically equivalent here. The fix is paired with a new bounded-time regression test (`tests/logger.spec.ts:2427`) that feeds a 100,000-character hostile input through `sanitizeSegment` and asserts the call finishes in under 1 s — a hard ceiling that any future regression to a quadratic-shaped regex would blow past. CWE-1333, CWE-400, CWE-730.
  - **`js/prototype-polluting-assignment` — MEDIUM severity — `src/request-middleware.ts:449` (alert #2).** The bracket assignment `target[finalKey] = REDACTED` at the bottom of `redactEntryPath` was the surface CodeQL flagged. The function ALREADY had a deny-list guard that ran over every segment of the user-supplied `redactPaths` path before any traversal, returning early if any segment matched `__proto__` / `constructor` / `prototype`. With that guard in place the bracket assignment was safe in practice, but the single upstream loop checked all segments together — including the final one — so CodeQL's local taint flow could not statically prove that `finalKey` was never a forbidden key by the time it reached the assignment. Restructured the guard into two stages that protect distinct concerns: part 1 — the upstream loop now iterates `segments[0..length-2]` (intermediate segments that get traversed via `cursor[segments[i]]`, where a forbidden key would land the cursor on `Object.prototype` itself); part 2 — a new inline check immediately before the assignment that gates the final segment. Both stages reference the existing `FORBIDDEN_OBJECT_KEYS` set (single source of truth across the package — header accumulators, `redactValue` rebuild, and now `redactEntryPath`). Behavior is unchanged: every prototype-pollution path that returned early before still returns early now, just from a different (and CodeQL-visible) statement. Added a focused regression test (`tests/request-middleware.spec.ts:1577`) that mounts a `JSON.parse('{"__proto__": "real"}')` object as the assignment cursor and walks a path whose ONLY forbidden segment is the final one — the harder case where the upstream `hasOwnProperty` filter would NOT short-circuit on its own, so the new inline guard is the load-bearing check. CWE-1321, CWE-78, CWE-79, CWE-94, CWE-471, CWE-915.
  - **`js/incomplete-sanitization` — HIGH severity — `scripts/release-tag.mjs:88` (alert #1).** The CHANGELOG excerpt extractor built its regex from `pkg.version.replace(/\./g, "\\.")` — a hand-rolled escape that handled only `.` and left `\`, `+`, `*`, `?`, `^`, `$`, `{`, `}`, `(`, `)`, `|`, `[`, `]` un-escaped. The runtime exposure is essentially zero because `pkg.version` is read from `package.json` and is always a plain SemVer string (the upstream `release:prepare` script that mutates it runs `npm version`, which validates the input), but a hand-edited or compromised `package.json` with a metacharacter in the `version` field would produce a malformed regex or one that matched more than intended. Replaced the single-char escape with the standard MDN-documented regex-metachar escape `s.replace(/[.*+?^${}()|[\]\\]/g, "\\$&")`. The release-tag script is a Node CLI with no surrounding test harness, so the CodeQL alert closing on the next scan is the verification signal. CWE-20, CWE-80, CWE-116.

### Tests (2026-05-12)

- **`tests/logger.spec.ts` — new `sanitizeSegment` bounded-time test.** `it("sanitizeSegment handles long runs of hyphens in linear time (ReDoS regression)")` pins the linear-time invariant against a 100,000-character hostile input. Asserts both the time bound (`< 1000 ms`) and the behavioral invariant (`"x"` survives, the leading hyphen run is stripped). Lives inside the existing `property-based — sanitizeSegment / buildLogFilePath` describe block.
- **`tests/request-middleware.spec.ts` — new `redactEntryPath` final-segment guard test.** `it("redactEntryPath final-segment guard catches __proto__ as an OWN property on the cursor")` exercises the new part-2 guard with the harder JSON-parsed-`__proto__` case where the `hasOwnProperty` filter would NOT short-circuit on its own. Asserts the own `__proto__` key on the cursor is left untouched, the cursor's prototype chain is unchanged, and `Object.prototype` is unpolluted globally. Lives inside the existing `prototype-pollution hardening` describe block.
- Test count: 251 → **253** (+2). Coverage remains 100% on statements, branches, functions, and lines across `errors.ts`, `logger.ts`, `redact.ts`, and `request-middleware.ts`.

## [0.21.6] - 2026-05-12

### CI / Workflow Hardening (2026-05-12)

- **GitHub Actions versions refreshed to current latest majors across all workflows** (`.github/workflows/ci.yml`, `.github/workflows/release.yml`, `.github/workflows/codeql.yml`). All bumps are Node 24 runtime refreshes with no input/output schema changes — the workflows continue to consume identical YAML inputs and produce identical artifacts, status checks, and release outputs. GitHub-hosted runners have shipped Node 24 support since runner `v2.327.1` (Aug 2025), so the matrix legs (Ubuntu / Windows × Node 18 / 20 / 22 / 24) and the release job all continue to schedule on first-attempt with no runner pinning required. The full pre-completion checklist passes (build, 251/251 tests + 100% coverage on every metric, lint, type-check, format:check) and `npm audit` continues to report 0 vulnerabilities at every audit level. Runtime + dev npm dependencies are already pinned to their respective `latest` dist-tags as of the prior 0.21.5 cut — no `package.json` dep movement in this release; the `package-lock.json` change is the version-line bump only.
  - `actions/checkout`: `v4` → `v6` (runtime: Node 20 → Node 24; credential persistence moved to a separate file in `v6.0.0`).
  - `actions/setup-node`: `v4` → `v6` (runtime: Node 20 → Node 24; `v5` introduced opt-in `packageManager`-field auto-detection but we already pass an explicit `cache: "npm"` input, so behavior is unchanged; `v6` narrowed the auto-detect to npm only).
  - `actions/upload-artifact`: `v4` → `v7` (runtime: Node 20 → Node 24 across `v5`/`v6`; `v7.0.0` migrated the action package to ESM internally and added an opt-in `archive: false` direct-upload mode that we do not use — the default zipped behavior is preserved).
  - `codecov/codecov-action`: `v5` → `v6` (runtime: Node 20 → Node 24; no input changes — `token`, `files`, `flags`, `slug`, and `fail_ci_if_error` continue to flow through unchanged).
  - `softprops/action-gh-release`: `v2` → `v3` (runtime: Node 20 → Node 24; the upstream maintainer documents `v3` as a pure runtime cut with no `with:` input changes — `tag_name`, `name`, `body`, `draft`, and `prerelease` continue to flow through unchanged).
  - `github/codeql-action/{init,analyze}`: `v3` → `v4` (runtime: Node 20 → Node 24; the GitHub Security maintainers schedule `v3` for deprecation in December 2026, so moving to `v4` retires that countdown ahead of the deprecation window — `languages`, `build-mode`, `queries`, and `category` continue to flow through unchanged).

### Dependencies (2026-05-12)

- **npm direct dependencies — no changes required.** Every direct dependency listed in `package.json` was re-checked against its npm `latest` dist-tag and confirmed already-pinned to the current latest: runtime (`winston@3.19.0`, `winston-daily-rotate-file@5.0.0`, `moment-timezone@0.6.2`) plus all 18 devDependencies (`typescript@6.0.3`, `eslint@10.3.0`, `jest@30.4.2`, `ts-jest@29.4.9`, `tsup@8.5.1`, `prettier@3.8.3`, `@types/node@25.7.0`, etc.). `npm update` (which bumps transitive deps to the latest version satisfying each parent's range) produced no lockfile diff. `npm audit` reports 0 vulnerabilities at every audit level.

## [0.21.5] - 2026-05-12

### CI / Repo Hardening (2026-05-12)

- **Codecov coverage upload + badge** (`.github/workflows/ci.yml`, `codecov.yml`, `README.md`): New `codecov/codecov-action@v5` step on the Ubuntu / Node 22 leg of CI uploads `coverage/lcov.info` (Jest emits this by default; `coverageReporters` includes `lcov` out of the box). Authenticated via the `CODECOV_TOKEN` repo secret, tagged with the `unittests` flag, with `fail_ci_if_error: false` so a Codecov outage cannot redden a passing build. A new `codecov.yml` mirrors the Jest `coverageThreshold` (100% on every metric) but marks both the project and patch status checks as `informational: true` — Codecov posts the delta comment and the badge updates, but the Jest gate remains the single blocking source of truth on coverage. The `ignore` list pins coverage reporting to `src/`, matching Jest's `collectCoverageFrom`. New `codecov` badge added to the README header alongside the existing npm / License / CI / CodeQL badges.
- **Repository rulesets** (applied via GitHub API — no source change): A `main-branch-protection` ruleset (id 16297868) now enforces PR-only merges with squash as the sole allowed method, all 8 CI matrix legs (`Validate (Node 18.x|20.x|22.x|24.x / ubuntu-latest|windows-latest)`) plus the CodeQL `Analyze (javascript-typescript)` check as required status gates, strict-mode "branch must be up to date before merging", linear history enforcement, and explicit blocks on force-push and branch deletion. A second `release-tag-protection` ruleset (id 16297879) covers the `refs/tags/v*` glob with blocks on tag deletion, tag move (update), and non-fast-forward operations so a published release tag is effectively immutable. Both rulesets list `RepositoryRole: Admin` (actor_id 5) as a bypass actor with `bypass_mode: "always"` so the maintainer retains escape-hatch access for genuine emergencies without weakening the routine guardrails.
- **Repository settings hardening** (applied via GitHub API — no source change): Merge button restricted to squash-only (rebase and merge-commit disabled) with `PR_TITLE` as the squash commit title and `PR_BODY` as the body, so the linear-history rule above is never blocked by an attempted rebase/merge. Auto-merge and the "Update branch" button enabled so PRs that go green can self-land without manual button-mashing. Auto-delete branch on merge enabled. Wiki disabled (the project documents architecture in `CLAUDE.md` and `README.md`, not the wiki).
- **Security feature enablement** (applied via GitHub API): Confirmed `secret_scanning` and `secret_scanning_push_protection` are enabled (push-time block on committed credentials, already on from a prior session). Enabled `private_vulnerability_reporting` so security researchers can disclose privately via GitHub's UI rather than the public issue tracker. Enabled `vulnerability-alerts` for read-only CVE notifications on production dependencies; Dependabot version-bump PRs are intentionally NOT enabled (out of scope per global instruction — the project uses manual semver-safe dep refreshes documented in CHANGELOG instead).
- **README badge row expanded** (`README.md`): Added `npm provenance` (static badge linking to the npm package page where the SLSA v1 attestation lives) and `Dependencies` (Libraries.io release status for `@hiprax/logger`) alongside the existing npm / License / CI / CodeQL / codecov / Node.js badges. Order is now: npm version → npm provenance → License → CI → CodeQL → codecov → Dependencies → Node.js.

## [0.21.4] - 2026-05-12

### CI / Release (2026-05-12)

- **Tag-triggered npm publish with provenance** (`.github/workflows/release.yml`): New workflow fires on `v*.*.*` tag pushes (and `workflow_dispatch` with a tag input). Runs the full quality gate set (`lint`, `type-check`, `format:check`, `test`, `build`), verifies the tag matches `package.json` `version` (refuses to publish on mismatch), runs `npm pack --dry-run` for tarball sanity, then `npm publish --provenance --access public` over OIDC (no long-lived publish secret on the wire). Finally extracts the matching `## [X.Y.Z]` block from `CHANGELOG.md` and creates a GitHub Release with those notes as the body via `softprops/action-gh-release@v2`. Concurrency is grouped on `release-${{ github.ref }}` with `cancel-in-progress: false` so a release run is never preempted mid-publish. Replaces the previous `publish.yml` which triggered off a manually-published GitHub Release.
- **Hardened CI workflow** (`.github/workflows/ci.yml`): Matrix expanded to Node `18.x / 20.x / 22.x / 24.x` (Node 24 added) across `ubuntu-latest` and `windows-latest`. Added `permissions: contents: read` (least-privilege), grouped concurrency (`ci-${{ github.ref }}`, cancel-in-progress), and added a `Runtime audit` (`npm run audit:runtime`) + `Verify tarball contents` (`npm pack --dry-run`) step on the Ubuntu / Node 22 leg. Coverage artifact retention remains at 14 days.
- **CodeQL static analysis** (`.github/workflows/codeql.yml`): New workflow analyzing `javascript-typescript` with the `security-and-quality` query pack. Runs on push to `main`, pull requests targeting `main`, and on a weekly schedule (`0 6 * * 1` UTC). Build mode `none` (no TypeScript build needed for analysis).
- **Issue + PR templates** (`.github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE.md`, `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/{bug_report,feature_request,config}.yml`): Bug report template captures package version, Node version, OS, affected surface (createLogger / createRequestLogger / createNoopLogger / shutdown / Types / Other), reproduction (rendered as TypeScript), expected vs actual behavior, and additional context. Feature request template captures problem, proposed solution, alternatives, and context. Blank issues disabled via `config.yml`. PR template enumerates the five-step pre-completion checklist mandated by the project CLAUDE.md.
- **Release helper scripts** (`scripts/{_lib,verify,release-prepare,release-tag,sync-main,new-branch}.mjs`): Zero-dependency Node scripts that automate the release ritual. `npm run verify` runs all five quality gates with a clean per-check pass/fail summary; `npm run release:prepare -- <bump>` bumps `package.json`, promotes `## [Unreleased]` to `## [X.Y.Z] - YYYY-MM-DD` in `CHANGELOG.md`, commits on `release/vX.Y.Z`, and pushes the branch (with PR URL printed for the user); `npm run release:tag` verifies state then creates and pushes the annotated `vX.Y.Z` tag that triggers the release workflow. `npm run sync` fast-forwards `main` and prunes branches whose remote was deleted; `npm run branch -- <prefix> <slug>` creates a conventionally-named feature branch from fresh `origin/main`. Wired into `package.json` `scripts`.

### Dependencies (2026-05-12)

- **All dependencies refreshed to the latest published versions on npm** (`package.json`). All bumps stay within the existing major versions — semver-safe with no migration required, no public-API changes, and no test changes. Every upgrade ran the full pre-completion checklist (build, 251/251 tests + 100% coverage on every metric, lint, type-check, format:check) and `npm audit` continues to report 0 vulnerabilities.
  - **Runtime:** no changes — `winston@3.19.0`, `winston-daily-rotate-file@5.0.0`, and `moment-timezone@0.6.2` are already on their respective `latest` dist-tags.
  - **Dev — patch/minor:**
    - `@types/node` 25.6.0 → 25.7.0
    - `@typescript-eslint/eslint-plugin` 8.59.2 → 8.59.3
    - `@typescript-eslint/parser` 8.59.2 → 8.59.3
    - `typescript-eslint` 8.59.2 → 8.59.3

### Dependencies (2026-05-04)

- **All dependencies upgraded to latest versions, including major-version bumps for `typescript`, `eslint`, `@eslint/js`, and `@types/node`** (`package.json`, `tsconfig.json`). Every upgrade ran the full pre-completion checklist (build, test 251/251 + 100% coverage, lint, type-check, format:check) and `npm audit` continues to report 0 vulnerabilities. Backward compatibility preserved — no public API changes, no test changes required.
  - **Runtime — patch/minor:**
    - `moment-timezone` 0.6.0 → 0.6.2
    - `winston` 3.18.3 → 3.19.0 (no breaking changes; same major)
  - **Dev — patch/minor:**
    - `@types/express` 5.0.5 → 5.0.6
    - `@typescript-eslint/eslint-plugin` 8.56.0 → 8.59.2
    - `@typescript-eslint/parser` 8.56.0 → 8.59.2
    - `typescript-eslint` 8.56.0 → 8.59.2
    - `jest` 30.2.0 → 30.3.0
    - `prettier` 3.6.2 → 3.8.3
    - `ts-jest` 29.4.5 → 29.4.9
  - **Dev — major:**
    - `typescript` 5.9.3 → 6.0.3 — Required adding `"ignoreDeprecations": "6.0"` to `tsconfig.json` because `tsup`'s internal DTS sub-build sets `baseUrl: "."` (deprecated in TS 6, removed in TS 7) regardless of the user's tsconfig — this is upstream in `tsup@8.5.1`'s `dist/rollup.js:6837` and out of our control. `ignoreDeprecations` is the TypeScript-team-sanctioned compatibility shim documented at https://aka.ms/ts6 and surfaced in the TS 6 error message itself. Our own `tsconfig.json` does not use any of the TS 6 deprecated options (`baseUrl`, `outFile`, `moduleResolution: classic`, `target: es5`, `esModuleInterop: false`); we already explicitly set `types`, `strict`, `target: ES2020`, and `moduleResolution: Bundler`, all of which are TS 6-compliant. No source code changes were required.
    - `eslint` 9.39.1 → 10.3.0 (paired with `@eslint/js` 9.39.3 → 10.0.1) — ESLint 10 drops Node.js 18, 19, 21, and 23 (now requires Node ≥ 20.19.0), removes the legacy `.eslintrc.*` config (we already use flat config in `eslint.config.mjs`, so no change), changes the `eslint.config.*` lookup to start from each linted file's directory, and tracks JSX references for scope analysis. None of these changes affected our flat config, our rule set, or our source code — lint passed with zero warnings on first run. The package's user-facing `engines.node: ">=18.0.0"` is intentionally unchanged because runtime consumers are unaffected; the new floor only applies to contributors running `npm run lint` locally.
    - `@types/node` 24.10.1 → 25.6.0 — drop-in compatible; no source code changes needed.
- **`tsconfig.json`**: Added `"ignoreDeprecations": "6.0"` (see TypeScript 6 entry above for rationale).

### Fixed (2026-05-04)

- **Pretty-mode logger no longer crashes when metadata or message contains `BigInt` values** (`src/logger.ts`): The three `JSON.stringify(...)` calls inside `formatMessage` (the message stringification on the non-string branch, the stack stringification, and the metadata block stringification) lacked a BigInt-safe replacer. A caller-supplied payload such as `logger.info("Order", { orderId: 123n })` therefore threw `TypeError: Do not know how to serialize a BigInt` synchronously back at the caller, taking the surrounding code path down with it. Added a small `bigintSafeReplacer` helper — `(_key, value) => typeof value === "bigint" ? value.toString() : value` — and threaded it through every `JSON.stringify` call in the pretty-mode pipeline. The non-string `info.message` branch additionally short-circuits a bare `BigInt` message via `info.message.toString()` so `logger.info(123n)` renders as the literal `123` instead of routing through `JSON.stringify`. String coercion (rather than emitting a JSON number) is the conservative choice — JSON numbers are IEEE-754 doubles, so values above `Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER` (snowflake / Twitter X-style IDs, large order IDs) round-trip with precision loss; the `"123"` string form preserves fidelity end-to-end and matches the convention already in use by `logform/json.js`'s built-in replacer (which `winston.format.json()` uses internally — JSON-mode output already string-coerces BigInts via that replacer, so no change was needed in the JSON branch). `bigintSafeReplacer` is exposed via `__loggerInternals.bigintSafeReplacer` for direct test access.
- **`tests/logger.spec.ts` — new `describe("BigInt-safe serialization", ...)` block** (7 new tests): pretty-mode metadata BigInt does not throw and renders as `"orderId": "123"`; JSON-mode metadata BigInt parses cleanly with `parsed.orderId === "123"` (pins the existing logform replacer contract from the consumer side); pretty-mode BigInt-as-message renders as the literal `123` (newline-bounded regex guard); JSON-mode BigInt-as-message round-trips as `parsed.message === "456"`; pretty-mode walks nested BigInts inside metadata objects (`{ outer: { inner: { id: 999n } } }` → `"id": "999"`); pretty-mode walks BigInts inside arrays (`{ ids: [1n, 2n, 3n] }` → all three string-coerced); direct unit coverage on `__loggerInternals.bigintSafeReplacer` proves it string-coerces BigInts (positive, zero, negative) AND identity-passes every other type (string, number, `null`, `undefined`, plain object). Test count grew 244 → 251 (+7). Coverage stays 100% on every metric across `errors.ts`, `logger.ts`, `redact.ts`, `request-middleware.ts`.

### Fixed (2026-05-04)

- **Built-in object types (`Date`, `Map`, `Set`, `RegExp`, `URL`, `Error`, `Buffer`) are now preserved through redaction instead of being silently emptied to `{}`** (`src/redact.ts`): `redactValue` previously walked every non-array object via `Object.entries(...).reduce(..., {})`. Built-in classes expose their data through non-enumerable accessors (`Date#getTime`, `Map#entries`, `Buffer#toString`, the `RegExp` source/flags getters, the `URL` href accessor, etc.), so `Object.entries` returned `[]` for them and the rebuild produced the empty object `{}` — losing the original instance entirely. Affected ANY consumer who set `LoggerOptions.maskMetaKeys` (the metadata redactor on the logger pipeline) OR `RequestLoggerOptions.includeRequestBody: true` (the middleware's `serializeBody` calls `redactValue` unconditionally — even when `maskBodyKeys` is empty), so real-world payloads carrying timestamps, IDs as `Map`, sets of tags, regex patterns, or `URL` instances all collapsed to `{}` in the log line with no warning. Added an "is plain object" guard at the top of the rebuild branch — `const proto = Object.getPrototypeOf(value); if (proto !== null && proto !== Object.prototype) { return value; }` — so built-ins, custom-class instances, typed arrays, `Promise`s, and anything else with a non-`Object.prototype` chain pass through untouched. Plain objects (`{}`, `Object.create(null)`) continue to recurse for key-based masking; arrays continue to recurse element-by-element; the FORBIDDEN_KEYS deny-list (`__proto__` / `constructor` / `prototype`) from Iteration 2 remains in effect on the recursion path. Resolves Iteration 3 audit task #1.
- **`tests/request-middleware.spec.ts` — new `describe("redactValue preserves built-in object types …", ...)` block** (12 new tests): direct `redactValue` calls on `Date` / `Map` / `Set` / `RegExp` / `URL` / `Error` / `Buffer` / a custom-class `Widget` instance assert `out === input` AND `out instanceof <Built-in>` AND a representative content accessor (`getTime()`, `get(...)`, `has(...)`, `source`/`flags`, `href`, `message`, `toString("utf8")`); a nested-built-ins-inside-plain-object case verifies the outer shape was rebuilt while every built-in field retains its original instance identity AND the surrounding plain object continues to recurse; an `Object.create(null)` plain-object case verifies the `proto === null` branch still recurses with key-based masking applied; a `serializeBody` end-to-end case verifies a `Date` survives intact alongside a `secret` redacted by `maskBodyKeys`; a final `serializeBody` case proves `Map` / `Set` / `RegExp` / `URL` / `Error` / `Buffer` ALL survive intact when nested in the body. Test count grew 232 → 244 (+12). Coverage stays 100% on every metric across `errors.ts`, `logger.ts`, `redact.ts`, `request-middleware.ts`.

### Added (2026-05-04)

- **`LoggerOptions.format?: "pretty" | "json"`** (`src/types.ts`, `src/logger.ts`, `src/errors.ts`): New option (default `"pretty"` for backward compatibility) that swaps both the file pipeline and the console pipeline for a structured-JSON emit suitable for log shippers like Datadog, Loki, ELK, Splunk, and Vector. When `format: "json"`, the pipeline becomes `winston.format.combine(buildTimestampCapture(clock), winston.format.errors({ stack: true }), buildMetaRedactor(maskMetaKeys), winston.format.json())` — every log call writes one JSON object per line (NDJSON / JSON-Lines) carrying `level`, `message`, `timestamp` (the captured-at-call-time UTC `"YYYY-MM-DD HH:mm:ss"` string), an `errors({ stack: true })`-resolved `stack` field for `Error` payloads, plus any caller-supplied metadata merged at the top level. The new internal `buildMetaRedactor()` format runs the shared deep `redactValue` primitive over `info` (excluding `level`, `message`, `timestamp`, `stack`, and Symbol-keyed slots) BEFORE `json()` serializes — so `maskMetaKeys` redaction continues to apply to JSON output too. The console pipeline emits the SAME raw JSON in `"json"` mode (no colorize) so a sidecar shipper that scrapes `stdout` sees byte-identical payloads on every transport. Validated synchronously via the new `validateFormatOption()` helper; an unknown value (e.g. `"JSON"`, `"plain"`) throws `LoggerOptionError({ code: "INVALID_FORMAT" })` BEFORE the registry cache lookup, and the `format` field participates in the conflict-warning signature so a second `createLogger()` call with a divergent value for the same cache key surfaces the conflict. README gains a new "JSON Output for Log Shippers" section with worked Datadog Agent, Promtail (Grafana Loki), and Filebeat (ELK) ingestion examples; new error code `INVALID_FORMAT` documented under `LoggerOptionErrorCode`. Resolves Iteration 2 audit task #14.
- **`createNoopLogger()` export** (`src/logger.ts`, `src/index.ts`): New stateless silent-logger factory that returns a SINGLETON winston-Logger-shaped Proxy whose every log-level method (`error`, `warn`, `info`, `http`, `verbose`, `debug`, `silly`, generic `log`) and every `EventEmitter` lifecycle method (`end`, `close`, `on`, `once`, `removeListener`, `addListener`, `removeAllListeners`, `emit`, etc.) is a no-op returning the logger for chainability. The Proxy also surfaces `level: "silent"`, a frozen empty `transports` array, and a forward-compatible chainable no-op for any unknown winston method (so future winston releases that add new methods do not throw). Designed for libraries / SSR pipelines / tests that want a silent default without taking on a Winston `[winston] Attempt to write logs with no transports` warning, without minting transports / file handles, and without registering with the internal logger registry (the singleton is shared, NOT cached — `resetLoggerRegistry()` does not affect it). Drop-in compatible with the request-logging middleware's `logger` option. README gains a new "Silent / No-op Logger for Libraries & SSR" section with library-default and middleware-integration examples. Resolves Iteration 2 audit task #15.
- **`tests/logger.spec.ts` — 17 new tests across two new describe blocks** (`describe("format option (JSON output)", ...)` × 8 and `describe("createNoopLogger", ...)` × 8, plus 1 internals coverage test): JSON branch round-trip (parse output line, assert canonical `level`/`message`/`timestamp` shape and metadata round-trip), `maskMetaKeys` redaction applies to JSON output (deep nested-object redaction), `Error` payloads emit `stack` field via `errors({ stack: true })`, default `format` remains `"pretty"` (regression guard with `JSON.parse` failure assertion on the first line), `INVALID_FORMAT` error for unknown format strings, `validateFormatOption` no-op for undefined, `validateFormatOption` accepts every documented value, `buildMetaRedactor` pass-through when no maskMetaKeys configured, `buildMetaRedactor` preserves reserved info keys (`level`/`message`/`timestamp`/`stack`); `createNoopLogger` singleton identity across calls, level-method silent emission (spies on `console.log/error/warn/info` confirm zero output), lifecycle-method chainability (`end()`/`close()`/`on()`/`once()`/`removeListener()` all return the logger), `level: "silent"` and frozen empty `transports` array surface, registry isolation (`resetLoggerRegistry()` does not affect the singleton), forward-compat chainable no-op for unknown winston methods, Symbol-keyed access returns undefined (not thenable / iterable), drop-in compat with `createRequestLogger({ logger: createNoopLogger() })`. `__loggerInternals` now exposes `buildMetaRedactor`, `validateFormatOption`, `VALID_FORMATS`, and `RESERVED_INFO_KEYS` for direct test access.

### Changed (2026-05-04)

- **`README.md` — request-middleware section relabelled "HTTP Request Logging Middleware (framework-agnostic)"** (and the corresponding TOC anchor): clarifies that the middleware consumes the local `LoggableRequest` / `LoggableResponse` shapes and never imports `express` — Fastify, Koa, raw Node `http`, or any adapter that produces matching shapes works the same way. A new lead-in paragraph documents the `peerDependencies.express` entry as documentation-only (it signals the most common host framework but imposes no runtime coupling). The `peerDependencies` / `peerDependenciesMeta` blocks in `package.json` are intentionally retained — Express is by far the dominant host framework and the `optional` marker means non-Express consumers see no install warning. Resolves Iteration 2 audit task #11.
- **`README.md` — `process.hrtime.bigint()` Node-version note** added to the request-start-symbol section (one sentence): `process.hrtime.bigint()` requires Node 10.7+, well below the package's `engines.node: >=18.0.0` floor. Resolves Iteration 2 audit task #12.
- **`CLAUDE.md` — test count refreshed** from `195` to the current `215` in the architecture comment under `tests/`. Resolves part of Iteration 2 audit task #13.

### Maintenance (2026-05-04)

- Cumulative test suite now sits at **215 tests** across 2 spec files (`tests/logger.spec.ts` + `tests/request-middleware.spec.ts`); coverage remains 100% on all metrics (statements, branches, functions, lines) across `errors.ts`, `logger.ts`, `redact.ts`, `request-middleware.ts`. Older CHANGELOG bullets that quote intermediate counts (`168`, `195`, `210 → 215`) are preserved as historical snapshots of the post-batch state at the time they were written. Resolves Iteration 2 audit task #13.

### Added (2026-05-04)

- **`LoggerOptions.escapeMessageNewlines?: boolean`** (`src/types.ts`, `src/logger.ts`): New opt-in option (default `false` for back-compat) that mitigates the log-injection class of attack where untrusted user input concatenated into a `logger.info(...)` call writes fake `[ERROR]` entries via embedded newlines. When `true`, the printf rewrites every `\r` / `\n` in a string-typed `info.message` as the literal escape sequences `\\r` / `\\n` BEFORE the message is concatenated into the rendered log line. A user-supplied payload such as `"alice\n[ERROR] (admin)\nfake event"` now renders as one log entry with the visible `\n` escapes inside the body, instead of three lines that an analyst, log parser, or SOC tool may treat as authentic. Non-string messages are unaffected (they already round-trip through `JSON.stringify`, which escapes newlines). Wired through `formatMessage` (new `FormatOptions.escapeMessageNewlines` flag, threaded into both the file and console pipelines so the escape applies on every transport) and added to `buildOptionsSignature` so a second `createLogger()` call with a divergent flag for the same cache key surfaces the conflict warning. README "Security Notes" section gains a new bullet documenting the threat model with a worked example. Resolves Iteration 2 audit task #6.
- **`tests/logger.spec.ts` — new `describe("escapeMessageNewlines", ...)` block** (3 tests): (1) end-to-end via the file pipeline — `logger.info("line1\nline2")` with the option on renders as the literal `line1\nline2` substring with no real newline survival inside the body; (2) regression guard — without the option, the existing raw-newline behavior is preserved (back-compat); (3) direct internals call on `__loggerInternals.formatMessage` proves the option flag wires through the printf for both `\r` and `\n` (`"alpha\r\nbeta"` → `"alpha\\r\\nbeta"`).

### Fixed (2026-05-04)

- **`truncateString` is now Unicode-safe — surrogate pairs (emoji, astral-plane code points) survive intact** (`src/request-middleware.ts`): Switched the truncation primitive from `value.slice(0, maxLength - 1) + "…"` (which operates on UTF-16 code units) to `Array.from(value).slice(0, maxLength - 1).join("") + "…"` (which iterates by Unicode code points). The previous implementation could land a truncation boundary inside a UTF-16 surrogate pair, producing a lone high surrogate that renders as the Unicode replacement character (e.g. `truncateString("ab😀😀", 4)` returned `"ab\uD83D…"` — a torn-in-half emoji). The new implementation honors emoji and astral-plane code points; `truncateString("ab😀😀", 4)` now returns `"ab😀…"` (4 code points; first emoji preserved intact, no lone surrogate). The JSDoc on `truncateString` documents the contract precisely: counts Unicode CODE POINTS (not UTF-16 code units), but does NOT count GRAPHEME CLUSTERS — a base character followed by a combining mark (`a` + U+0301) is two code points; consumers who need grapheme-correct slicing should reach for `Intl.Segmenter` (Node 18+). Resolves Iteration 2 audit task #7.
- **`tests/request-middleware.spec.ts` — 2 new tests** for `truncateString`: (1) emoji surrogate-pair survives intact across three `maxLength` cases (`4`, `5`, `6`), with a defensive regex guard asserting the output never contains a lone high surrogate; (2) the documented combining-mark caveat is exercised — `"ábc"` (composed `a` + combining-acute + `b` + `c`) truncated to `maxLength=2` yields `"a…"` (the base character without the combining mark), proving the contract that the helper counts code points (not graphemes).

### Changed (2026-05-04)

- **`tests/_helpers.ts` — new `withEnv(key, value, fn)` helper** standardizes the env save/restore pattern across the request-middleware test suite. The helper saves `process.env[key]`, sets it to the supplied `value` (or `delete`s the key when `value === undefined`) for the lifetime of the synchronous `fn` callback, and restores the original value (or `delete`s the key if it was unset before the call) in the `finally` block. Closes the latent bug where the previous unguarded `process.env.NODE_ENV = originalEnv` cleanup would write the literal string `"undefined"` into `process.env.NODE_ENV` when the test was invoked from a shell that had not exported `NODE_ENV` (jest defaults `NODE_ENV` to `"test"` in normal runs, which is why this is dormant in CI but bites local `npx jest --no-cache` invocations). Migrated 8 unguarded blocks plus 4 already-correctly-guarded blocks across `tests/request-middleware.spec.ts` (lines 464-492, 494-522, 524-550, 552-580, 1297-1307, 1313-1337, 1339-1353, 1355-1377) so the entire test file now speaks with one voice on env hygiene. The `_helpers.ts` JSDoc documents the `undefined`-coercion bug the helper exists to prevent. CLAUDE.md "Testing Patterns" section updated. Resolves Iteration 2 audit task #10.

### Maintenance (2026-05-04)

- **Test suite expanded** from 210 → 215 tests (+5 net), 100% coverage maintained across `errors.ts`, `logger.ts`, `redact.ts`, and `request-middleware.ts`. Version bumped to `0.20.0` (minor) — new `escapeMessageNewlines` option is API-additive and the `truncateString` change tightens behavior without breaking the public contract (still returns a string of length `<= maxLength` measured in code units, ending in the ellipsis).

### Fixed (2026-05-04)

- **Behavior fix — `validateRotationStrategy` now rejects rotation configs the upstream library silently disables** (`src/logger.ts`, `src/types.ts`): The previous lenient `ROTATION_SHAPE_PATTERN = /^\d+(b|k|kb|m|mb|g|gb|d)?$/i` was applied to BOTH `rotation.maxSize` AND `rotation.maxFiles`, accepting many inputs that `winston-daily-rotate-file`'s internal parsers would silently drop. End result: `createLogger({ rotation: { maxSize: "20mb" } })` passed our validator but produced a logger whose size-based rotation was disabled (`getMaxSize("20mb")` returns `null`); `createLogger({ rotation: { maxFiles: "20m" } })` produced a logger whose retention was silently coerced to "20 files" (via `parseInt("20m") === 20`). Replaced the single shared regex with two field-specific patterns mirroring the upstream contract exactly: `MAX_SIZE_PATTERN = /^(?:0\.)?\d+[kmg]$/i` (single-letter `k`/`m`/`g` suffix, optionally `0.`-prefixed for fractional values like `"0.5m"`) and `MAX_FILES_PATTERN = /^\d+d?$/i` (bare numeric file count or day-suffixed retention window). `validateRotationField` now takes the appropriate pattern per field and emits a tailored error hint enumerating the valid suffixes for that field. `RotationStrategy.maxSize` and `RotationStrategy.maxFiles` JSDoc in `src/types.ts` document the upstream contract precisely with accepted/rejected examples. `__loggerInternals` now exposes `MAX_SIZE_PATTERN` / `MAX_FILES_PATTERN` (the legacy `ROTATION_SHAPE_PATTERN` export is removed). **Behavior fix: previously-broken rotation configs now throw early at logger creation** with `LoggerOptionError({ code: "INVALID_ROTATION" })` instead of producing a logger whose rotation has been silently disabled. Newly-rejected inputs include `maxSize: "20mb"` / `"100b"` / `"1gb"` / `"20"` (bare number) / `"20d"`, and `maxFiles: "20m"` / `"20kb"` / `"1g"`. Resolves Iteration 2 audit task #3.
- **`tests/logger.spec.ts` — rotation validation tests refactored** (4 blocks, +3 net tests): The previous `"accepts valid rotation shapes"` block (which asserted `"7"`, `"500"`, `"20m"`, `"20M"`, `"200k"`, `"1g"`, `"1gb"`, `"14d"`, `"100b"` ALL pass for both `maxSize` AND `maxFiles`) was split into two field-specific blocks: `"accepts valid rotation.maxSize shapes"` covers `"20m"`, `"100k"`, `"1g"`, `"0.5m"`; `"accepts valid rotation.maxFiles shapes"` covers `"7"`, `"14d"`, `"30"`. Two new blocks assert `INVALID_ROTATION` is thrown for previously-tolerant garbage: `"rejects rotation.maxSize values upstream silently disables"` covers `"20mb"`, `"100b"`, `"20d"`, `"20"`; `"rejects rotation.maxFiles values that carry a size suffix"` covers `"20m"`, `"20kb"`. No existing tests outside the rotation block needed updating — every other rotation usage already used the now-canonical forms (`"2d"`, `"5d"`, `"7d"`, `"14d"`, `"30d"`, `"20m"`).

### Fixed (2026-05-04)

- **Proxy deny-list expanded to cover JS engine introspection probes** (`src/logger.ts`): Added `"name"`, `"prototype"`, `"length"`, `"arguments"`, `"caller"`, and `"bind"` to `DENIED_PROXY_PROPS`. Without these entries the proxy's `FALLBACK_METHOD_NAME_PATTERN` accepted them as legitimate method names and minted a no-op shim that emitted a spurious `Unknown logger method "name"` warning when reflective code paths probed the logger (e.g. `obj.constructor.name`, `Function.length` arity sniffing, prototype walks, `.bind(...)` rewrappers). They now return `undefined` like the other engine/framework probes already in the deny-list. **Resolves Iteration 2 audit task #2.**
- **`awaitTransportFlush` listener leak on timeout** (`src/logger.ts`): The per-transport `finish`/`close` `once` listeners installed by `awaitTransportFlush` were never removed when the timeout side of `Promise.race([flushAll, timeout])` won. For long-running processes that called `shutdownLogger` repeatedly with timeouts (test loops, supervised restarts, k8s liveness probes), the transports accumulated dead listeners — each one closing over the `resolve` slot of a never-fulfilled promise — eventually emitting EventEmitter's `MaxListenersExceededWarning`. The helper now returns `{ promise, cleanup }`; `shutdownLogger` calls `cleanup()` inside the outer race's `.finally()` so the listener pair is detached regardless of which side wins (success path is a documented no-op since `settle()` already removed them). The `settle` closure uses TypeScript's definite-assignment assertion (`let settle!: () => void`) instead of a placeholder arrow so coverage reflects only code paths that actually execute. **Resolves Iteration 2 audit task #5.**
- **ANSI strip regex includes the leading escape character** (`src/logger.ts`): The strip pattern in `formatMessage` previously embedded a raw 0x1B byte directly in the source via the regex literal — runtime-correct but fragile (any tooling that normalizes whitespace/control chars would silently break it). Replaced the raw byte with the `\x1b` escape sequence (`/\x1b\[[0-9;]*m/g`) and added an inline comment explaining that the leading ESC is required so the strip pass actually removes the codes instead of leaving the bare ESC byte behind. **Resolves Iteration 2 audit task #8.**
- **`warnedMethods` Set is bounded** (`src/logger.ts`): Added `MAX_TRACKED_UNKNOWN_METHODS = 50` and a cap-and-reset pass in `emitUnknownMethodWarning` mirroring the existing `MAX_TRACKED_TRANSPORT_ERRORS` pattern. Without the cap the per-logger Set grew monotonically when a misbehaving consumer funnelled arbitrary input into method names (e.g. `logger[req.headers["x-action"]]()`). When the Set reaches 50 entries it is cleared so genuinely new typos after a long run still surface a warning. The constant is exported on `__loggerInternals` for test access. **Resolves Iteration 2 audit task #9.**
- **`tests/logger.spec.ts` — 4 new tests** for the four fixes: (1) proxy returns `undefined` for `name`/`prototype`/`length`/`arguments`/`caller`/`bind` with no spurious `console.warn`, (2) `formatMessage` direct-call test passes `info.level = "\x1b[31merror\x1b[39m"` and asserts the rendered token is `[ERROR]` with no surviving 0x1B byte, (3) emit `MAX_TRACKED_UNKNOWN_METHODS + 1` unique unknown-method names and assert the (MAX + 1)th still warns (cap-and-reset triggered) and the first method name can warn again after the reset, (4) `shutdownLogger` timeout-rejected path: instrument the awaiter via `__loggerInternals.awaitTransportFlush` and assert `cleanup()` removes both `finish` and `close` listeners by exactly one each (without touching winston's pipeline-owned listeners). One additional unit test verifies `awaitTransportFlush` exposes a `cleanup()` that detaches both listeners from a bare transport.

### Security (2026-05-04)

- **Prototype-pollution hardening across `redactValue`, `redactEntryPath`, `applyHeaderMask`, and `normalizeHeaders`** (`src/redact.ts`, `src/request-middleware.ts`): Closed a prototype-pollution gap in the deep-redaction primitive and the surgical `redactPaths` walker. (1) `redactValue` (the shared primitive used by `LoggerOptions.maskMetaKeys` and `RequestLoggerOptions.maskBodyKeys`) now skips own keys named `__proto__` / `constructor` / `prototype` during the rebuild. Without the skip, the `acc[__proto__] = …` assignment invoked the prototype setter and corrupted the local result's prototype chain (and silently dropped sibling keys downstream of the offending entry). (2) `redactEntryPath` (the `redactPaths` walker) now bails out as a no-op when ANY path segment matches the same deny-list. Without the guard, a consumer-supplied path like `redactPaths: ["body.__proto__.toString"]` walked into `Object.prototype.toString` and overwrote it with the literal string `"[REDACTED]"`, polluting every object in the process. (3) `applyHeaderMask` and `normalizeHeaders` (both the normalize loop and the allow-list-filter loop) now skip the same three keys when rebuilding the header bag. Hand-built header fixtures or non-standard adapters that surface a `__proto__` own property no longer mutate the result's prototype chain. The deny-list is centralized as `FORBIDDEN_KEYS` (in `src/redact.ts`) and `FORBIDDEN_OBJECT_KEYS` (in `src/request-middleware.ts`) so the package speaks with one voice on prototype-pollution hygiene. No CVE filed; internal hardening only. **Resolves Iteration 2 audit task #1, #4.**
- **Six new prototype-pollution regression tests** (`tests/request-middleware.spec.ts`): A new `describe("prototype-pollution hardening", ...)` block under `request middleware internals` covers (a) `redactEntryPath` no-op for paths containing `__proto__` / `constructor` / `prototype` (asserts `Object.prototype.toString` is unchanged after the call), (b) `serializeBody` on a JSON-parsed `__proto__` payload returns an object with `Object.prototype` (or `null`) prototype and no `polluted` global, (c) `normalizeHeaders` skips `__proto__` on input header bags, (d) `normalizeHeaders` allow-list path also rejects `__proto__` in the allow-list, (e) `normalizeHeaders` mask-set path skips `__proto__` (defense via the upstream normalize loop), (f) direct `applyHeaderMask` call on a `__proto__`-laced bag (defense-in-depth via the inner deny-list). A `logger maskMetaKeys probe` test exercises the end-to-end pipeline with a `JSON.parse('{"username":"alice","password":"topsecret","__proto__":{"polluted":true}}')` payload through `serializeBody` and asserts no global pollution. Defensive `afterEach` deletes `Object.prototype.polluted` after every case so a regression cannot cascade. `applyHeaderMask` is now exposed via `__requestInternals` for direct test access.
- **Out-of-process probe against the built dist tarball** confirms no global prototype pollution observable through the public API: `Object.prototype.polluted === undefined` and `Object.prototype.toString` remains the original function after every exploit attempt (`logger.info` with `__proto__` metadata, `createRequestLogger` with `redactPaths: ["body.__proto__.toString"]`, `createRequestLogger` with header bag carrying `__proto__` as own property).
